beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create a slash command, add a command, write a custom command, define command arguments, or use CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT — the Command Development skill fires. Commands are not agents. Commands are frequently-used prompts defined as Markdown files that Claude executes when invoked. Three locations: project commands in .claude/commands/, personal commands in ~/.claude/commands/, plugin commands in plugin-name/commands/. Five frontmatter fields: description, allowed-tools, model, argument-hint, disable-model-invocation. The critical rule: commands are instructions FOR Claude, not messages TO the user. Let me walk you through the structure, the fields, and where this skill gets it right and where it bites.

props.command: Create a slash command for my plugin that reviews a pull request and posts a summary.
output: Commands = frequently-used Markdown prompts Claude executes
Critical rule: body = instructions FOR Claude, not messages TO user

beats.act: anatomy — locations + frontmatter fields + dynamic args
beats.narration_text: A command file is a Markdown file with an optional YAML frontmatter block. Three locations. Project commands in .claude/commands/ — scoped to the specific project, shown as '(project)' in /help, the right choice for team workflows. Personal commands in ~/.claude/commands/ — available across every project on the machine, shown as '(user)', for personal cross-project utilities. Plugin commands in plugin-name/commands/ — bundled with the plugin, shown as '(plugin-name)', discovered automatically when the plugin is installed. Five frontmatter fields. description: a brief string shown in /help — defaults to the first line if omitted. allowed-tools: which tools the command can use — Bash(git:*) not Bash(*) is the correct pattern. model: haiku for fast simple commands, sonnet for standard, opus for complex analysis — defaults to the conversation. argument-hint: documents expected arguments for autocomplete and /help display. disable-model-invocation: prevents programmatic SlashCommand tool calls — set true when manual-only. Dynamic arguments: $ARGUMENTS captures all input as one string, $1 and $2 and $3 are positional, @file includes file contents inline, and !backtick bash backtick executes a shell command to inject dynamic context.

props.sparkLine: Location sets scope. allowed-tools sets blast radius.

beats.narration_text: The instructions rule is the most critical design constraint. Commands are consumed by Claude, not by the user. Correct: 'Review this code for SQL injection, XSS attacks, and authentication issues. Provide specific line numbers and severity ratings.' Incorrect: 'This command will review your code for security issues. You will receive a detailed report.' The first tells Claude what to do. The second describes what will happen to the user — Claude gets nothing actionable. Always write the command body as a directive to Claude. Four common patterns. Review pattern: allowed-tools Read plus Bash(git:*), inject changed file list with !backtick git diff dash dash name-only backtick, then explicit review criteria. Testing pattern: Bash(npm:*), run tests with !backtick npm test dollar-one backtick, analyze results and suggest fixes. Documentation pattern: @$1 includes the source file — no bash needed, just a clear documentation directive. Workflow pattern: Bash(gh:*) plus Read, numbered phases for a complete PR lifecycle. For plugin commands, ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} resolves the plugin's absolute installation path — use it to reference scripts, configs, and templates portably without hardcoded paths.

props.sparkLine: The body is Claude's directive. Write it that way.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Command Development gets the instructions-versus-description distinction right — commands are written for agent consumption, explicitly stated and illustrated with a correct-versus-incorrect pair showing the contrast. Three locations are completely specified with scoping rules, /help labels, and appropriate use cases. Dynamic argument syntax is comprehensive: $ARGUMENTS, positional $1/$2/$3, @file references, and !backtick bash backtick all covered. allowed-tools scoping patterns are concrete — Bash(git:*) versus Bash(*) is the right pattern and shown with examples. Organization guidance — flat versus namespaced — is paired with the threshold of fifteen commands, so the decision is not just advisory. Where it bites: bash execution syntax is pushed to an external reference file — references/plugin-features-reference.md — and the exact syntax is not reproduced in the skill. $IF() conditional syntax appears in an example but is never explained. There is no validation tooling — no script to check a command file's structure or test that it fires. CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT is documented without cross-platform notes on path format. And disable-model-invocation is listed but its operational effect on the SlashCommand tool is not explained.

props.sparkLine: Instructions rule solid. Bash detail: bring the reference.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Command Development fires for slash commands, custom commands, command frontmatter, dynamic arguments, and CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT usage. Commands: Markdown files, YAML frontmatter optional. Three locations: project, user, plugin. Five fields: description, allowed-tools, model, argument-hint, disable-model-invocation. Critical rule: command body is instructions FOR Claude, not messages TO the user. Args: $ARGUMENTS, $1/$2/$3 positional, @file references, !backtick bash backtick inline. Patterns: Review, Testing, Documentation, Workflow. Plugin path: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for portable plugin-relative references. Gaps: bash syntax in external ref, $IF() unexplained, no validation tooling, no cross-platform CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT notes.

artifactLines: Commands = Markdown files Claude executes · YAML frontmatter optional
5 fields: description · allowed-tools · model · argument-hint · disable-model-invocation
Critical rule: body = instructions FOR Claude, not messages TO the user
Gaps: bash syntax external · $IF() unexplained · no validation tooling

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Create a slash command for my plugin that reviews a pull request and posts a summary. Watch four things: first, does the command body start with instructions Claude can act on — not a description of what the user will see? Second, does allowed-tools use Bash(gh:*) or Bash(git:*) rather than Bash(*) or the bare star? Third, is argument-hint set — something like [pr-number] — so the autocomplete hint shows up in /help? And fourth, does any bash execution use the !backtick syntax inline rather than a placeholder the user would need to fill in? If the command body reads like a user-facing announcement, the skill's critical rule was missed. If allowed-tools is Bash(*), the principle of least privilege was dropped. Those are your gates.

props.command: Create a slash command for my plugin that reviews a pull request and posts a summary.
props.runningText: Watch: body = Claude instructions? allowed-tools scoped? argument-hint set? bash inline?
output: Expected: body starts with 'Review PR #$1 for...' not 'This command will...'
Expected: allowed-tools: Bash(gh:*) or Bash(git:*) — not Bash(*)
Red flag: no argument-hint, or body reads like a user message

beats.narration_text: Command Development. Liam, in for Bear.
